title: Trade Ministry makes further clarification on the implementation of reference value regarding petrochemical imports
bodyHtml:
Amid concerns regarding the determination of which exchange price to consider for certain imports of petrochemical products, the General Directorate of Customs issued a letter addressing the matter.

In the letter, it is highlighted that:

In commercial activities, goods can vary in terms of the country of origin and the country of trade. Contracts between buyers and sellers can contain differing provisions regarding terms of purchase and sale.

Within this context, when evaluating the declared value for such imported products, it's important to take into account the type of exchange/price specified in the contract between the buyer and the seller, based on explanations and information provided by the obligator, in addition to the country from which the goods originate or the country of trade.



 MINISTRY OF TRADE

The General Directorate of Customs

Number :E-85593407-165.04

Subject: Reference value

 

02.08.2023 / 87751685

TO CUSTOMS AND FOREIGN TRADE REGIONAL DIRECTORATES

It has been observed from incidents reported to our Ministry that there have been uncertainties regarding which exchange price to consider for certain imports of petrochemical products as published in the Value Data Bank available on our Ministry's portal.

As is known, the examination of declared values by customs authorities can involve the use of reference/data information. Reference/data price information, although not used as the customs value of imported goods by customs authorities, serves as data that guides the examination of the accuracy and truthfulness of the declaration. These data provide guidance on whether a detailed examination of the declaration is necessary.

In this context, under the heading "Petrochemical Products" in the Value Data Bank available on our Ministry's portal, exchange/price types (NWE, RDAM, SE ASIA, ARA) related to the relevant products are published, indicating the exchange prices.

However, it's important to note that in commercial practices, the origin country of the goods and the country of trade can vary, and different provisions regarding terms of purchase and sale can be included in contracts between buyers and sellers.

Therefore, when evaluating the declared value for such imported products, it is crucial to take into account not only the country from which the goods originated or the country of trade but also the exchange/price type specified in the contract between the buyer and the seller based on explanations and information provided by the obligator.

Please consider this information as provided.
